data.title: Intel: Why The Alphabet Collaboration Is A Bigger Signal Than It Looks
data.description:
Thesis

Intel Corporation (INTC) has just expanded its partnership with Alphabet/Google (GOOG). For me, it signals that Xeon CPUs are not likely to be displaced in the AI era, but rather they seem to be becoming more deeply embedded at the core of hyperscale infrastructure, as I’ll explain. It's a good trend for Intel, and the collaboration would imply that they’re evolving from a component supplier into a strategic systems partner. They’re set to co-design balanced architectures alongside custom IPUs, which should position them to benefit directly from scaling AI demand.

Elsewhere, the valuation would reflect a turnaround already being priced in. So in that sense, this deal would then reinforce the durability of CPU demand and support the long-term earnings recovery story here. I last covered Intel towards the end of last year, in which I explained what the Apple (AAPL) collaboration over the 18A node meant for investors. I see this collaboration as another step in the right direction for Intel's recovery and would remain Buy-rated again.

Intel and Google continue their collaboration

As you know, Intel and Google have just recently expanded their partnership. I see it as a pretty subtle but important shift, specifically in how AI infrastructure is evolving for both parties. You see, much of the recent AI boom has been focused on GPUs and specialized accelerators. And it is true, many companies have seen huge share momentum from expanding their GPU portfolio. However, this collaboration reinforces that CPUs are still the backbone of large-scale systems here.

On Intel’s side, it comes down to their Xeon processors. These processors basically orchestrate AI workflows in that they combine a very strong general-purpose compute with system-level control capabilities. This is what accelerators lack. Architecturally speaking, the Xeon can offer us much higher core counts and large shared caches. They also have wide vector engines, specifically the AVX-512 and the AMX for AI. Elsewhere, they run high memory bandwidth with DDR5, which is what allows them to very efficiently handle data preprocessing tasks like decoding, filtering, and batching. Now, these tasks are always in demand with the accelerating AI momentum, but they’re also usually operations that carry irregular logic and heavy memory access that GPUs struggle with.

So in that sense, they also act as the control plane of AI systems. And to bring this back to real-world deployments like Google Cloud, Xeons will essentially prepare and route data for large-scale training. They will also be particularly useful for latency-sensitive services that Google runs, like search/recommendations. They handle request processing and can even run smaller inference workloads using AMX, which should avoid unnecessary accelerator use. So overall, this results in better utilization of expensive hardware and lower latency. It sounds complicated, but if we just keep in mind that Google wants their offerings like Google Cloud, Search, YouTube, and its Gemini to be faster, more efficient, and a bit cheaper to run, we can see what they’re getting from this collaboration.

Advancing AI Infrastructure with Xeon

We also have to take into account here that Google is continuing to rely on Xeon across their cloud instances. They, of course, have the C4 and N4, which tells me that even as accelerators still dominate headlines, general-purpose compute is still extremely essential for balancing performance/latency and also cutting costs. What I mean by this is that we should take it as a signal that, despite the AI boom being centered on GPUs/TPUs, Intel’s Xeon CPUs are still very much embedded in the core of Google’s infrastructure here. There will be a stable demand for Xeon tied to every AI workload, not replaced by accelerators. For example, even when Google runs their Gemini models on TPUs, Xeon CPUs are still needed to preprocess the data and manage requests. They also have to coordinate that distributed training so as AI usage grows, CPU demand can scale alongside it rather than being displaced.

Elsewhere, we have the deepening co-development of custom ASIC-based IPUs to consider. It seems there’s a growing emphasis on offloading infrastructure-level tasks away from CPUs. So that means there is a push toward custom ASIC-based IPUs, and I see it as a shift in how hyperscale systems are being optimized in 2026. You see, instead of having CPUs handle everything, some specific infrastructure tasks, like networking, storage management, encryption, and, again, data movement, are being offloaded to dedicated chips designed for those exact functions. The whole environment, in this sense, is moving towards micromanagement and efficiency.

We know that CPUs are flexible, but they’re not actually the most efficient at these kinds of repetitive, high-throughput tasks. So hyperscalers would rather offload them to IPUs to reduce overhead and latency. In doing this, it also frees CPU cores, which can then focus on higher-value work like orchestration and AI pipeline management, as I explained earlier. Now, in theory, an IPU could handle packet processing, storage I/O, and security isolation independently. So this should improve consistency and utilisation across a massive data centre fleet.

I’m mentioning the specifics of this trend since it’s highly relevant to the Intel/Google collaboration here. Essentially, it tells me that they want to co-design a balanced system, and not just improve standalone chips. Xeon CPUs are pairing with custom IPUs here, so Google can scale AI infrastructure a lot more efficiently. While Xeons remain the control layer, the IPUs will take over the plumbing of the data center. For Intel, this is clearly good news, as per the 5% share price jump. It definitely deepens their role beyond just selling CPUs and more into a strategic partner role in system architecture. Google sees them as essential in reducing the total cost of ownership and improving performance at scale.

Valuation is the risk

As for a fair valuation, the stock does seem to trade at a premium on some multiples. I see that the market is pricing in a significant earnings recovery here. The forward price-to-earnings sits at about 125x for 2026, well above the sector median in the 22x range, and alongside a forward non-GAAP PEG of 4.49x. So this tells me that even with expected growth, it still looks expensive on a near-term earnings basis. Over the last year, or essentially since Lip Bu Tan joined Intel, the stock price has been up about 187%. It's heavily outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY), as you can see below.

Revenue-based multiples like EV to sales sit at 6.2x, and price-to-sales at 5.3x are also nearly double the sector medians, which is pretty notable here given Intel’s relatively modest revenue growth profile compared to their high-growth AI peers. However, these multiples do compress quite sharply over time, with the consensus estimates suggesting a drop to about 23x price to earnings by 2029. This would be driven by pretty aggressive EPS growth expectations, which include over 100% growth in 2027. So it would seem that the market is betting heavily on a successful turnaround here, specifically margin expansion, AI-driven demand, and much improved capital efficiency from their manufacturing/foundry strategy.

I find it hard to disagree with the market here in that sense. The current mid-$61 stock price does seem expensive considering the run-up in price over the last year, but in short, Intel is being valued less on what it is today and more on what it is expected to become over the next 3 to 5 years. That upside case would then justify the current level.

Looking ahead

Just to sum up, I would remain Buy-rated at current levels on Intel, since this partnership simply reinforces my core thesis here in that Xeon CPUs are not being displaced anytime soon in the AI era. Instead, I see them as becoming more deeply embedded within increasingly complex, hyperscale systems, which is good news for Intel. Intel is shifting more toward a strategic infrastructure partner, co-designing balanced architectures with IPUs rather than simply supplying components. This should help their long-term positioning in AI spending going forward. And yes, the valuation already reflects a pretty meaningful turnaround and leaves a lot less room for error in the near term, but the durability of CPU demand alongside accelerating AI workloads would make the case for a longer-term earnings recovery story.

data.title: March CPI: Gas Prices Surge The Most On Record, Real Wage Growth Drops
data.description:
CPI inflation surged in March, rising 0.865%, matching estimates, as an oil-driven energy shock led the way. It was good enough for a top-10 biggest sequential CPI hike since 1970. While headline inflation jumped, core CPI increased a more modest 0.196%, a tick under economists’ projections, with food prices flat.

Energy spiked 10.9% (the largest rise in 21 years), driven by a sharp 21% rise in gasoline (the biggest surge on record, back to 1967). Gasoline accounted for almost three-quarters of the inflation spike. On an annual basis, headline CPI is 3.3%, while the core rate is 2.6%.

Digging into the report, core goods rose just 0.1%, with new vehicles flat, used cars down 0.4%, and apparel up 1%. Core services also increased 0.2%, led by shelter (+0.3%), including rent (+0.2%) and OER (+0.3%), while hotels rose 0.2%. Medical care and car insurance were flat, though airfares climbed 2.7%. Big picture, super core CPI rose 18 basis points last month, which was an improvement from +35 basis points in February. Also keep in mind that the partial government shutdown may have shaved some pricing pressure.

For the consumer, real average hourly earnings are now a paltry +0.3% on a trailing 12-month basis, down sharply from 1.4% in February. Higher energy bills weigh on households, with electricity CPI up 4.6% over the past 12 months and gas utilities up 6.4% vs March 2025. Food-at-home CPI is +1.9%, but food away from home is hotter at +3.8%. The good news is that vehicle prices are up a weak 0.5% YoY, with used vehicle prices off 3.2%.

Equity futures were little changed after the 8:30 a.m. ET data, while the bond market largely yawned at the March numbers. The bulls are fine with that, as the S&P 500 (SP500) looks to put in a second consecutive large weekly climb. As for Fed Fund futures, there was a modest uptick in expectations for an eventual rate cut. Of course, the FOMC is likely to be in a holding pattern for quite some time.

The onus is now on the bulls to carry the S&P 500 to its 8th straight positive session.

March CPI Close to Consensus, Stocks Inch Higher

March CPI +0.9% as expected ... biggest jump since June 2022

CPI YoY now 3.3%, highest since April 2024

Core CPI up just 0.2%, below estimates

Core CPI YoY 2.6%, up MoM, but below estimates

Core goods CPI at +1.2% y/y in March vs. +1.0% prior. Core services +3.0% y/y vs. +2.9% prior

S&P 500 futures up a touch

Yields Little Changed

62% Implied Chance of at Least 1 Fed Rate Cut This Year

Looking ahead, more key data hits next week. UMich will be interesting today, given much-higher prices at the pump, and then Tuesday’s March PPI report offers a fresh look at wholesale inflation trends.

Before that, housing data hits Monday, with March Existing Home Sales, while NFIB Small Business Optimism on Tuesday morning could point toward continued optimism in hiring intentions from Main Street companies (recall that March ADP employment was quite strong among small- and medium-sized businesses).

Later, Empire Manufacturing for April, along with the Fed’s Beige Book, offers clues on the state of regional economic conditions. Jobless claims, Philly Fed, and Industrial Production round out the data week, with Friday’s calendar all clear. Bank earnings will create potential volatility in the Financials sector, too.

Data Deck

Bank earnings on tap

The Bottom Line

March CPI came in close to estimates, with the headline rate matching forecasts, while the core rate was a touch light. Stocks and bonds took the data in stride, with all eyes fixed on the conflict in Iran. Earnings season ramps up next week, too, with plenty of data catalysts on tap.

data.title: LyondellBasell: North America's Cost Advantage Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction

LyondellBasell (LYB) has been a great winner since I first started to cover the chemical firm which has appreciated a total of 36% since my first Buy rating and over 10% since my last review. LYB and its other peer Dow (DOW) have seen huge financial stress over the past couple of years and the tide seems to be finally turning. I’ll dive into why I believe the firm could continue to win.

Current Dynamics

The main bullish argument has been based on the conflict in the Middle East, as this instability has created immense volatility in the energy and feedstock markets that have put a priority on US-based chemical companies. See, as companies outside of North America produce ethylene and propylene, they rely on naphtha, in which the price is correlated with crude oil, that has been a real thorn for European and Asian manufacturers. On the other hand, LYB’s assets in North America are mainly powered by NGLs, mainly ethane, which is decoupled from crude oil prices. This essential divergence has created a massive margin windfall for the company’s US operations as the global price for PE and PP is set by the high-cost producers in the rest of the world.

Data shows that European ethylene prices reached $1.21 per kilogram due to these high energy price dynamics. Though on the NA side, prices remain significantly lower at 53 cents per kilogram, translating to pure margin capture for LYB, as for every $100 per metric ton increase in the price of PE, the company will generate around $320MM in additional annualized EBITDA. Secondly, while the firm’s European assets have faced higher costs, they have also been able to operate without Middle Eastern competition as exports have been trapped behind the Strait of Hormuz. These two dynamics have allowed LYB to implement aggressive price increases in Europe to protect margins with spot prices increasing by nearly 40%.

As LYB closed its Houston refinery, that was a significant source of earnings volatility, it is now pivoting the assets toward being the Houston Hub for Circular and Low Carbon Solutions. LYB will convert existing hydrotheaters and infrastructure for use in recycling technology. LYB is transforming a liability into a high-growth asset aligned with the future regulatory mandates. The hub will also include a massive plastic waste sorting facility and a second MoReTec advanced recycling plant, making LYB the dominant player in the circular economy in the US Gulf Coast. I find this approach relatively healthy for the firm as it is utilizing a foreclosed site that was already causing a lot of pain to the business and turning it into potentially one of the greatest green assets in North America.

Management also highlighted how polypropylene is a sleeping giant that is finally beginning to stir. PP margins have generally trailed PE due to oversupply and high propane feedsto costs, though the current environment has fundamentally changed this dynamic. Propane logistics has been heavily disrupted due to the situation in the Persian Gulf, which caused PDH operations in Asia and other high-cost regions to plummet to the mid-50% range. This global supply contraction in PP has coincided with a period of low domestic inventory, as US PE inventories were at just 37.7 days, well below the 45-day level typically required to balance the market. This tight supply and demand balance means that LYB will drive outsized earnings gains as pricing power returns to the PP segment.

On the European front, the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, which is now active in 2026, will serve as a double-edged sword that will ultimately protect LYB’s existing assets in the region from low-cost and high-carbon imports. By effectively taxing imports based on their carbon intensity, CBAM effectively raised the cost for competitors who rely on older and less efficient technologies, thereby preserving LYB’s market share. In the region where it is investing heavily in low-carbon solutions.

Valuation

On simple financial metrics, it is hard to estimate LYB’s value. It has an EV/EBITDA of 16.20, which stands at the middle of the peer range. As free cash flow also got depressed and the stock shot up, the yield is currently slim at 1.6%. I also did a scenario analysis for the company that, in a bull case, which I believe is currently in the cards considering the bullish dynamics, it should push the stock to an implied price of $95.34 per share. Though a source of skepticism has been S&P Global lowering the credit rating to BBB-, due to earnings and credit metrics that fell below previous expectations as the cyclical trough continued for longer. Though S&P expects EBITDA to improve to a range of $3.3B to $4.3B through 2028 as cost-saving initiatives take hold. LYB is also trading at a forward yield of 3.72%, one of the lowest levels since 2022.

One of the most critical growth prospects for the firm would be the Value Enhancement Program, which focuses on modernization and AI-driven predictive maintenance. The program delivered $1.1B in recurring annual EBITDA, plus management is expecting to extend that target to $1.5B in recurring annual EBITDA by 2028. LYB also needs to continue to showcase a high cash conversion rate to reflect the resilience of the LYB operating model. LYB achieved an exceptional 95% cash conversion rate and the firm is now targeting a long-term average of 80%. This high conversion allows the company to maintain its current dividend yield while simultaneously throwing billions into its CapEx program for the pivot.

Risks

The recent stock performance has been driven by the war premium created by the conflict in the Middle East, but if this rapidly de-escalates or the Strait of Hormuz fully opens, the temporary 10% tightening of global polyethylene supply would normalize, causing LYB to lose its North American cost advantage.

A massive wave of new production capacity coming mainly from China continues to depress global margins for polyethylene. S&P global notes that significant capacity is still slated to come online through 2027, which could effectively prolong this trough.

Conclusion

LyondellBasell has been surfing on a perfect storm after going through a tumultuous set of years that have hampered the financial footing of the firm. Though I’ll say that refining business exit as well as the dividend pivot have signaled that management is unafraid to make the hard decisions to preserve long-term value. I believe that the stock can go higher outside of the conflict in the Middle East. In that sense, I keep my Buy rating on LyondellBasell.

data.title: Gatekeeper Systems: Traders Losing Patience During Long Radio Silence
data.description:
I have been covering Gatekeeper Systems (GSI:CA) (GKPRF) on SeekingAlpha since summer 2024, when I first issued a bullish call on the stock in light of Gatekeeper's strong position in what was about to become a big market: upgrading passenger train locomotives with crash-hardened memory modules, as required by a Federal Railroad Administration [FRA] mandate. Basically, the federal government ruled that all locomotives of passenger trains must have security cameras by October, 2027. This should be easy to understand: planes have "black boxes" to help with post-mortem analysis after a crash, and this legislation just requires the same thing for trains. Since there are relatively few companies actively engaged in this market, and Gatekeeper has already installed these units for trains in Philadelphia's SEPTA system, they have been poised to capture an outsized portion of this ~$300m (according to the company) opportunity:

Shares went up about 600% last year as the first deals started to get announced, and Gatekeeper won a big one: Long Island Railroad [LIRR], for $27m CAD. Shares broke $2 USD last fall in the aftermath of that deal. Shortly after the results were announced, I sized up Gatekeeper's prospects in another article, urging readers to stay cautious, since:

the LIRR contract is just one data point, and we really still do not know how many more contracts Gatekeeper will win. There is very little visibility here, and Gatekeeper's management has not provided any form of clear guidance.

2026 News: Not Much So Far!

In the five months since that article appeared, shares have fallen by about half. According to my internet research, no other company has announced winning a contract for FRA security-camera / memory-module installations in a major metropolitan rail system. But then again - Gatekeeper has not had any major wins, either. This in itself is not a reason to buy or sell shares; the absence of news says nothing definitive about future prospects.

A second "update" was a $13.5m private placement, which closed November 13 and was followed by a ~12% sell-off in shares. The offering priced shares at about $1.50 USD, and so shares sold off to that level and then traded around it until about New Years.

The FY2026 Q1 results, released on January 29th, were also unexciting. These results disappointed some investors but told us nothing important about the key questions: (1) how many more FRA deals Gatekeeper will win, and (2) whether those deals will be followed by SaaS subscriptions.

In the last few months, the company has issued two press releases announcing about $1.8m CAD in new contract wins for school bus camera systems. These contracts are not really enough to move the needle. It is nice to see, however, that several of them included SaaS subscriptions.

I am more excited to see continued updates about Gatekeeper's OEM integrations. First came announcement of a proof-of-concept with L&T Technology Services " a global leader in AI, Digital & ER&D Consulting Services, for design and delivery of video solutions for freight rail" for a contract in the Middle East. Then, on March 5 came another announcement about an OEM integration with Coach & Equipment Bus Sales Inc.

FRA Mandate Opportunity

It is worth reiterating that in the LIRR bidding process, Gatekeeper only competed against two other potential providers (Railhead and Wi-Tronix). Gatekeeper offered a price about 40% below railhead and only marginally below Wi-Tronix. In other words, the only "real" competitor in this contract race was Wi-Tronix.

Wi-Tronix is private, so there is relatively little information available about them online, but according to the company website, Wi-Tronix already partners with Amtrak and several major rail providers. Here is some background:

  • 2017: receives strategic investment from Siemens.
  • September 2023: won a $1.6m grant from the FRA to install cameras on the Brightline/FEC transit corridor.
  • January 2024: won a pilot competition to partner with Metro-North and New Jersey Transit.
  • June 2024: announced a partnership with Siemens (SIEGY) Mobility to implement their "integrated data and communication system" across the LA Metro. It is somewhat unclear exactly what all this contract entailed, and its focus seemed to be more on "real-time train information" than on "safety and security measures" (which were also mentioned), but we might well imagine that LA will use Wi-Tronix for their FRA compliance upgrades since they are already using them for this.
  • October 2024: won a $20.5m USD FRA grant to upgrade data connectivity on short-line railroads.

Today, they aren't hiring, but I think this track record speaks to the fact that Wi-Tronix will be a very significant competitor for the remaining FRA contracts.

We will probably get another data point on this competition in the next few months, because the South Florida Regional Transportation Authority [SFRTA] currently has a RFP posted with a response deadline of May 7.

So if we conservatively estimate that the real FRA opportunity is something like $270m (about 10% less than what Gatekeeper management claims), and ~$20m of that has already been awarded to Gatekeeper for the LIRR, then there is about $250m remaining to be awarded.

Valuation and Recommendation

Using these figures, I model Gatekeeper winning *very roughly* 20-40% of the $250m in outstanding FRA contracts, for a total of about $50-$100m USD. The contract awards will likely be spread out over the next ~18 months. And then, judging by the LIRR revenue recognition timeline, arrive in stages over the following 1-3 years. This means that the full expected revenue of ~$75 million (the midpoint of $50m-$100m) will be spread out over the next ~4-5 years for an annual contribution of ~$10-$20m, ramping up over the next 12 months before peaking in FY28 (ending in Aug. 2028). (Specifically, I'm modelling very little revenue arriving in FY26, ~$10-$15m arriving in FY27, ~$30-$50m arriving in FY28, and then the remainder arriving in FY29-FY30.) Adding that on to Gatekeeper's current ~$30m revenue run rate, we come to a range of FY28 revenue expectations around $50-$70 million. Assuming roughly flat gross margins (which seems conservative given that Gatekeeper had to compete on price for the LIRR contract) and a modest amount of operating leverage, we get to a FY28 EPS of $0.05-$0.10 USD in EPS.

If we land at the high end of that range and many of Gatekeeper's FRA contracts lead to longer-running SaaS contracts, shares will appreciate considerably due to (1) higher SaaS margins and (2) the establishment of a stronger base of recurring revenue. But given that we are already almost halfway through FY26, it seems unlikely that much additional FRA revenue lands in this fiscal year. And it will take time for the contracts to be won and delivery to begin in FY27. So realistically, at current prices ($0.92 USD as of this writing), you are paying ~12x FY2028 earnings. For a company where most revenue is still coming from relatively low-margin equipment sales, and probably at least a year from posting solid profits, I don't think this is a crazy valuation. It prices in, at least in part, the significant remaining uncertainty about future FRA contract wins, the timing of revenue recognition, and the conversion of those wins to recurring SaaS revenue. I think Gatekeeper remains a "show-me" story.

FY2028 Bear Base Bull
EPS $ 0.05 $ 0.08 $ 0.10
P/E 15 (hardware) $ 0.75 $ 1.13 $ 1.50
P/E 20 (software) $ 1.00 $ 1.50 $ 2.00

SeekingAlpha categorizes Gatekeeper as an IT company, but in practice most of their revenue still comes from relatively low-margin equipment sales, and so I think we should be conservative about expecting the market to pay a high multiple for what could very well be unsustainable earnings growth.

In terms of risks, I am not worried about dilution. Gatekeeper raised ~$20m USD last year through two rounds of financing. In the latest quarterly reported (from January 29), the company reported $18m CAD in cash, $17m CAD in other additional working capital, and $13.5m CAD in two undrawn credit facilities. Given that the company is near-breakeven, and operating cash flow has been positive, on balance, in the decade since the company went public, I don't see any need for additional financing unless the company wins even more large contracts, which would on balance be good for shareholders.

Looking at the company's history, shares have compounded at almost a 30% CAGR in the decade since the IPO. But that long-term trend has felt very different for investors, who have seen shares swing widely, routinely doubling or tripling before losing half or even four-fifths of their value in huge multi-year swings. We have now entered another downtrend. I won't pretend to know what will happen next, but I think the long-term trend is a sober reminder that shares could fall much further as investors get sick of waiting for big new contract wins.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Reasons I Am Avoiding Owning Peloton Interactive Just Yet
data.description:
Peloton Interactive, Inc.'s (PTON) fundamentals, as well as the challenging macroeconomic landscape, combined with the heavy insider selling, make me bearish on the company in the near term. Sales are declining, and the firm's bottom-line results are still negative, while shareholders are being more and more diluted every year. The poor consumer sentiment in the United States, along with the elevated energy prices—driven by the outbreak of the war in Iran earlier this year—is pressuring the firm both from the demand and cost sides. While the company is planning to expand by “going to the gym,” the rollout is not expected until late 2026, and therefore, I do not see any significant catalysts in the near term that could boost sales or earnings. For these reasons, I rate PTON's stock as a sell today.

1. Sales, earnings, and shares outstanding

In the most recent quarter, PTON's results disappointed. Total sales declined as a result of declining subscription revenue and connected fitness products revenue. It is actually not only the past three months but also the past six months when sales deteriorated.

The following table breaks down the revenue decline in percentage terms. We can see that connected fitness revenue declined by as much as 3.7% YoY, while subscription revenue fell by 1.9%. The decline in the connected fitness products was primarily driven by the lower-than-expected product sales to existing members and the 7% decrease in paid subscriptions.

When we break down the revenue geographically, we can see that the international segment—which is significantly smaller than North America—slightly grew, but the decline in North America more than offset this growth.

It is not only the topline results that disappoint. It is even more concerning that after so many years in the business, PTON is still generating net losses. Considering the declining product sales as well as the declining number of paid members, I do not see how it will be turned around in the near term.

While the fundamentals are not attractive, the fact that PTON is raising capital by diluting its shareholders is even worse. Over the past 5 years, the amount of common shares outstanding increased by more than 40%.

I understand that firms in the initial growth stage often raise equity capital, but I do not see Peloton growing at a rate that could justify this amount of dilution.

As long as the firm cannot grow its sales consistently and it cannot achieve consistent positive earnings, I do not see why PTON could be an attractive investment.

2. Macroeconomic headwinds

The macro landscape is also not making the business easier for PTON. Consumer confidence in the United States is still hovering around the 10-year low. Poor sentiment normally indicates that people are uncertain about their financial outlook and the health of the economy overall, prompting them to spend less on non-essential, discretionary, durable products—just like the ones PTON is selling. As long as the sentiment is weak, I believe there will not be a significant increase in demand for the company's products and services.

On the other hand, the outbreak of the war in the Middle East sent energy prices through the roof. While prices came down slightly recently due to the ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Iran, they remain elevated due to all the uncertainty surrounding the Strait of Hormuz. Elevated energy prices lead to higher transportation and manufacturing costs, which could bite into PTON's profits.

All in all, the current macro landscape is not favouring PTON's business, in my opinion.

3. Insider activity

Another concerning fact is the amount of insider selling in recent months. Or I should rather say, the lack of buying activity. While insiders can sell for a lot of different reasons, if they buy, it normally means that they trust the outlook or they find the valuation attractive. In recent weeks, none of the insiders bought shares.

One might say that despite the insider selling activity, the valuation appears attractive based on the EV/sales and price/sales multiples. Indeed, PTON is selling at a discount compared to the consumer discretionary sector median, as well as its own historic metrics, but this alone does not mean that the stock is cheap. The poor fundamentals and the dilution of the shareholders are important to consider when looking at the valuation multiples. And currently, PTON may just be a value trap, especially if they cannot reach sustained profitability.

For these reasons, I do not find the firm attractive from a valuation perspective.

Upside potential

While I focused on the topic of why I believe PTON is not a worthy investment candidate right now, there is one piece of news that we should look at, which could ignite growth towards the end of 2026. And this is the fact that Peloton is “going to the gym.” By expanding into the gyms, Peloton could expand its user and subscriber base significantly, which could potentially ignite growth. In general, I like the idea, and I believe this is the single biggest catalyst for them in 2026. I cannot get bullish on the stock, however, before we see how this is executed and how much it will drive sales, subscriber count, and earnings.

Conclusions

In the most recent three- and six-month periods, PTON's sales—both in terms of subscription and connected fitness products—declined. At the same time, earnings remained negative, and the existing shareholders were diluted.

The macro landscape is also challenging for Peloton's business, with consumer confidence remaining around the 10-year low readings and energy prices skyrocketing due to the geopolitical tension in the Middle East.

Heavy insider selling activity in the recent past raises further concerns.

The only upside potential I see in the near term is PTON expanding into the gyms, which could lead to high sales and a higher subscriber count.

All in all, however, I am currently bearish on PTON and assign it a sell rating.

data.title: Lumentum: You Could Be Very Late To The Party
data.description:
Lumentum: The Next Nvidia?

Networking stocks are now enjoying their Nvidia moment (NVDA), so to speak, and I reckon there are few that could proclaim such a notable achievement as that of Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE).

After notching a spectacular and eye-popping 1,400% rally over the past one year as I pen this write-up, I think it’s fair for new investors to ask whether it still makes sense to buy now?

Being blessed with a networking stack that has the potential of seeing the TAM go through the roof in the next few years, I could understand why the market hasn’t held back.

How often do we see companies expressing such confidence in their ability to tap into a market opportunity that could grow 40% CAGR through 2030?

Furthermore, the narrative is firmly supported by what we are seeing on the ground, right?

I mean, I don’t have to repeat that the US hyperscaler CapEx is already expected to hit $700B, and that’s just for this year.

Next year, things could get even more heady, and I haven’t seen signs that anyone is looking to pull back, despite the ongoing uncertainties in the Middle East.

Plus, even the AI foundation model companies such as OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), are going for each other's jugular, with an ambitious appetite for compute capacity far exceeding what they have now.

Big tech certainly wants more AI compute capacity, and infrastructure builders are poised to tap into the “optical evolution” from here. Look at what Meta (META) inked with CoreWeave (CRWV) just this week!

So much so that having a top notch AI networking stack is no longer optional, especially with the AI agents now expected to become mainstream.

Lumentum's AI Networking Platform Poised For Rapid Expansion

And next-gen architecture for scale-out, and scale-up will necessitate the participation of co-packaged optics, opening up another massive growth pillar for LITE.

And what truly represents Lumentum’s opportunity here is the ability to embed itself across various layers in the networking stack.

I believe this affords a clear trajectory to the multi-year roadmap in supporting the defense of its high-growth premium.

Investors who are betting on the big growth inflection based on Lumentum’s growth narrative can point to several underpinnings driving its prospects.

These traverse cloud transceivers, optical circuit switch or OCS, as well as the CPO opportunity.

I believe what strengthens LITE’s case is that its tech platform has been many years in the making, as it was primarily based on and evolved from its beginnings in telecoms.

Hence, the company draws upon deep domain expertise and years of proven execution and “now evolved those into data center and hyperscaler applications.”

From that vantage point, I view that this so-called evolution and hockey stick inflection isn’t a mere coincidence, but largely based on the company’s ability to finesse its way into the hyperscaler market with its networking technologies.

Of course the timing is important. I guess that probably explains why LITE has printed well in excess of 1,000% gains and hasn’t looked back, right?

Still, questions about whether these gains are sustainable are valid and should contemplated by those wanting to jump on board right now.

Lumentum: AI Agents As The Next Growth Pillar

Yet, we are getting the picture that the market has an insatiable desire for what Lumentum serves on its plates.

Especially since we all know that the advent and expansion of AI agents are becoming a unstoppable primary driving force for LITE, as highlighted by management in a recent March conference.

The key word, “now optics power compute,” cannot be further overstated.

Networking is a foundational infra layer for Nvidia’s (NVDA) AI chips, besides the CUDA software layer.

I guess we don’t even need to guess why Nvidia decided to invest $2 billion in Lumentum, as Nvidia seeks to make sure it can gobble up the indium phosphide capacity.

And that’s one aspect of LITE’s business that could expand at gangbusters speed through the rest of the decade.

Meaning, management wants us to believe that we are still in the early innings of Lumentum’s optical portfolio. Based on the numbers presented, I find it hard to refute that the story truly has strong underpinnings.

And just to make clear how significantly its revenue growth could inflect from these nascent opportunities, the company also added plans to build “a new manufacturing facility in Greensboro, North Carolina to produce advanced lasers for the world’s largest AI data centers.”

That facility is intended to “manufacture Lumentum’s InP-based optical products.”

And that’s obviously going to be accretive to LITE’s P&L, but by a relatively large margin, which is why it's monumental.

We are talking about generating $5B in annualized revenue here, and the ramp could take place from as soon as early 2028.

So these are part of the expansion forecasts that could very well bring Lumentum into one of the most exciting AI networking players in the next few years if they could execute and convert these gargantuan opportunities into revenue.

Lumentum: Earnings Growth Inflection Through 2028 And Beyond

Based on the revised forecasts, Wall Street has also started to bake in the optimism into its revenue trajectory through 2028, seeing revenue plausibly scaling the $7B mark by then.

Management has already noted through its guidance update that they are capable of building out a $2 billion quarterly revenue run when they ramp up the Greensboro facility.

So, I believe the market has already priced in some success, at the very least.

And that could see LITE’s adjusted EPS spike to the $23.5 level, right?

In case you didn’t realize, Lumentum posted just $2.06 in adjusted earnings per share for FY2025, and is expected to close at $7.79 for the current fiscal year.

Obviously, we shouldn’t assume that the market is dumb and hasn’t taken into account these potential upside surprises.

I think the bigger question is whether, if we look forward to FY2028’s adjusted EPS outlook, we need to ask ourselves whether they look overvalued.

LUMN: Expensive By Most Measures

While the forward EPS multiple of almost 75x is undoubtedly expensive by most measures, when we pan out to FY2028, that multiple drops to 38x.

However, when I parse it against the tech sector median of 22x, I think it’s clear a lot of hope and optimism is reflected, right?

Investors have given the benefit of the doubt to management in executing this multi-year ramp and expansion even though we are still two FYs away from the unveiled guidance.

Therefore, if you have very high confidence that LITE could continue expanding even more quickly to justify an almost 40x FY2028 earnings multiple through the decade, I think you need to be confident that buyers are still willing to bid for higher prices to take the advance even further.

Buy Or Sell LITE Stock?

This is where I think we must be careful not to let our emotions go haywire even as we laud the company's upside potential.

The secular growth themes supporting the AI picks and shovel plays have proven their resilience. Despite the ongoing Middle Eastern crisis due to the recent Iranian war, the bullish momentum has remained incredibly robust.

But, I've also observed that LITE's print appears to have been taken a bit way too far for my liking.

And the aggressive forward earnings multiples also corroborate this narrative that I'm assuming.

At this point, I have not seen anything that suggests a bearish reversal is imminent.

But I do think that the current momentum has taken it way above the long-term moving averages (blue and red lines in the chart), which could quickly unravel if execution were to falter.

So I think for now, it's better for investors who want to get on board to wait for a more meaningful retracement first, and reassess the consolidation zones later.

Rating: Hold.

data.title: CoreWeave: The Neocloud King Validated By Meta's Billions
data.description:
Great News For CoreWeave

I'm glad that I initiated my investment coverage of CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) stock in late December, when it was correcting sharply, with plenty of investors worrying about the firm's operational sustainability amid debt loads and potential depreciation risks. Since my bullish call, the stock has managed to gain over 23% while the whole market is still in red (-1%), and I still believe that this kind of overperformance is very likely to continue further because we have some great news that should support the positive narrative.

News #1 - Meta's Validation

On April 9th, we saw CRWV close higher, partly because of the market rally, but another reason was the announcement of a long-term agreement expansion with Meta Platforms (META). The partnership itself isn't something new, as Meta has been using CRWV's platform to scale its inference workloads since 2023, according to Reuters. But now, as CoreWeave is nearing the initial deployment of the Nvidia Vera Rubin platform (NVDA), Meta seems to want to secure some additional capacity ahead of others.

This is another example that leading companies are choosing CoreWeave’s AI cloud to run their most demanding workload.

Seeking Alpha citing CRWV's CEO

I believe this expansion is another validation of hyperscaler demand for CRWV's offerings. The deal was initially signed for up to $14.2 billion through 2031 back in 2023, and now, three years later, we see Meta's needs rising so much that they felt the need to secure another $6.8 billion beyond the initial capacity limit.

CoreWeave currently makes only about $5.13 billion in sales on a TTM basis, according to Seeking Alpha, targeting $12-13 billion in FY2026 revenues at $0.9-1.1 billion adjusted operating income. According to Synergy's study, FY2025 revenues for the entire neocloud niche were slightly over $25 billion, so it looks like CRWV's market share in the neocloud space is about 20%, based on my calculations - which is a lot. While I agree that Nebius (NBIS) and IREN Ltd (IREN) are catching up and starting to gain share, CoreWeave is likely to remain the main market player for at least the next few years. Throughout this period, the firm should enjoy the organic growth of the total addressable market as workloads grow almost exponentially across different end users.

In addition to Meta, CRWV has plenty of other capacity agreements and deals that are likely to see expansions as well. For example, they've already expanded their deal with OpenAI (OPENAI) last year by up to $6.5 billion to support training of advanced AI models. Now, assuming that we're shifting to the inference era - which is the reason why Meta expanded their contract, based on the press release explanation - OpenAI is likely to extend the deal size with CRWV, too. OpenAI has the financials for that after raising $120 billion recently. Also, looking at its mega-cap peers, Microsoft (MSFT) might do the same thing. So, in my understanding, Meta has just sent a validation signal to the entire industry and the customer base of CRWV - those who are listening are likely to approach CRWV and sign new deals or extend existing ones to match Meta's AI growth ambitions.

News #2 - GPU Rental Prices Validation

According to Semianalysis data, Nvidia's H100 GPU rental prices have surged by about 40% in just 6 months, and the researchers noted that the GPU rental capacity is sold out across all GPU types.

Customers are fighting to pay $14/hr/GPU for p6-b200 spot instances in AWS, some Neocloud Giants no longer sell single nodes, H100s are getting renewed at the exact same rate they were signed at 2-3 years ago and some H100 contracts are being renewed for 4 years though 2028. Hunting for even 8 nodes (64 GPUs) of H100s or H200s is not easy – half the providers we asked were completely sold out, and most providers will simply respond they have no capacity of Hopper GPUs coming off contract at al.

Seeking Alpha citing Semianalysis

With this news and the current trend in rental prices, I believe that the biggest risk to all neocloud providers has diminished. Skeptics have been questioning the sustainability of their business models for a while, and there were plenty of discussions regarding the depreciation risks of their acquired assets. Now it turns out that even H100s are starting to generate superior returns - not to mention Blackwell GPUs, which have very tight availability right now.

The researchers said they are hearing lead times for new Blackwell deployments now extending into June-July due to strong demand for open-weight models and the ongoing surge in inference demand.

Seeking Alpha News

In my view, CRWV's business model is effectively validated by this trend - the demand for the already built-out infrastructure is so strong it justifies all the expansion plans that CoreWeave has been guiding for, and it makes the abundance of cheap debt on the firm's balance sheet look reasonable. I see fewer risks to the current sales expansion consensus estimates now (in fact, the chances of seeing a higher-than-consensus top line in the coming quarters have risen with the higher rental pricing).

Recalling the projections made by Synergy Research, we see that CRWV's market share should be at about 16.5% by 2031, so the current consensus seems to correctly assess the other players' expansion plans. But if we assume that CRWV's market share shrinks to only 18%, it's still going to be a massive beat compared to what is currently expected for the firm's FY2031 revenues - $72 billion in sales versus the $65.9 billion projected. And the FY2031 P/S would drop to 0.67x, making CRWV look cheap in terms of its valuation for the long run.

CRWV Stock - More On Valuation

CoreWeave continues to finance its expansion externally, mainly through debt issuances in different forms. As of the latest moves on that front, we see that the firm announced plans to raise $3 billion via senior convertible notes, and then they priced a private offering of $1.75 billion aggregate principal amount of 9.750% senior notes due 2031, according to Seeking Alpha. As I noted previously, the firm's business model looks more stable now as a whole because GPU rental pricing isn't dropping as much as many people might have initially assumed. That's why I believe that CRWV's debt isn't representing as much of a credit risk as some skeptics still think.

Anyway, with the rise in debt, the enterprise value has been drifting away from the market cap for the past months:

This trend pushes me toward using EV/EBITDA ratios as the only viable option for making a valuation update on CRWV.

YCharts data shows us that for the current year, CRWV is projected to make $7.38 billion in EBITDA, while in 2 years from now, that figure is going to soar to almost $23.40 billion:

Given the GPU rental pricing trends and assuming they hold for the next few years as the demand side only expands - thanks to AI agents and rising workloads - I think we should add a 10% premium on top of today's 2-year forward EBITDA consensus.

Let's also assume that CRWV trades at just 3-4x range forward EV/EBITDA in the next 12 months, which is going to be a reasonable target for the level of growth investors are about to see.

So, the above consensus of $23.37 billion EBITDA turns into $25.71 billion (10% premium), and at 3.5x it's going to lead me to a target EV of $90 billion. Right now, CRWV trades at $75 billion, so I see an upside potential of ~20% from today's price levels in the next 12 months. Wall Street consensus sees 30%+, but that's just another bullish validation for my "Buy" thesis update on CRWV.

Risks To My Views

In my previous article on CRWV, I noted the firm's heavy dependence on just a couple of clients. For example, Microsoft used to account for about 67% back in December 2025, which was a lot. Now, I guess the situation is changing with Meta's contribution and OpenAI deal, in addition to possible other signs. But the lack of customer diversification is still a clear risk to CRWV's sustainability that we should always keep in mind.

Another risk, which is impossible to ignore, is CRWV's debt load. I might be underestimating the size of the credit risk following the news of rising GPU rental prices. I sincerely believe the credit risk is overblown, but simple math is hard to ignore - as Louis Gerard wrote in his recent article on CoreWeave, the firm is spending $2.6 for every $1 in revenue this year, and since most of the funding comes from debt financing, it's creating a very shaky situation for the company.

Conclusion

I believe the latest news around CoreWeave - both internal and external - has validated the bullish thesis. While the risks are clearly there, which is why I wouldn't recommend buying CRWV without proper due diligence, I think the company has a great future as it expands and the core pricing metrics remain consistently bullish in the era of inference and AI agents that drive up demand. CoreWeave looks 20% undervalued to me, so I remain bullish on it.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson Q1 Earnings Preview: Time To Sell The Rally?
data.description:
Introduction

Pharmaceuticals and medical devices giant Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) delivered a surprisingly strong performance over the last couple of months and after having remained rangebound in the $160s since 2021. JNJ stock is currently trading at approximately $240, so is up 17% year-to-date. The performance over the last twelve months of more than 60% (not including dividends) make JNJ stock sound more like a hyper-growth tech stock than a mature blue-chip.

I've covered Johnson & Johnson quite extensively here on Seeking Alpha (coverage overview here). I was quite astonished, though, when I realized my last article was published back in July of 2024. Back then, I had taken a good look at JNJ's (surprisingly) consistent earnings and growth and rated the stock a "buy".

In light of my last article, written almost two years ago (I still can't believe it), and because JNJ will report its first-quarter 2026 results on April 14 at 8:30 a.m. ET, it's time for an update.

In this article, I'll examine what analysts currently expect from JNJ for next Tuesday, but more importantly, I'll provide an update on the company's drug pipeline and growth prospects. I'll also assess how I currently view the stock from a valuation perspective. Since JNJ is one of my largest holdings and currently accounts for nearly 6% of my stock portfolio's value, I'll also discuss whether I'm considering rebalancing my position. This is, of course, an aspect that must be carefully weighed: It's all too easy to "cut the flowers and water the weeds"...

Johnson & Johnson: Q1 2026 Earnings Preview And A Fresh Look At Company Fundamentals

The current consensus for JNJ's earnings per share (EPS) in the first quarter of 2026 is $2.68, based on the expectations of the 18 analysts tracked by Seeking Alpha. The low estimate ($2.14) and the high estimate ($2.89) translate to a rather broad range of 28%. If JNJ were to report earnings per share of $2.68, this would represent a 3.3% decline compared to Q1 2025, but it would, of course, be quite myopic to focus on such a short-term performance. The consensus for full-year 2026 currently stands at $11.50 per share, representing 7.0% year-over-year growth - a rate to which long-term investors are likely well accustomed to and which is in line with management's midpoint guidance, as provided in January together with the results for Q4 and full-year 2025.

I will not go into detail about JNJ's earnings quality in this article, as this was the topic of my last analysis. In terms of earnings (and importantly also sales) reliability, it is a well-known fact that the company only very rarely delivers a surprise in either direction and typically "errs" to the upside on an annual earnings basis. Quarterly surprises are generally not worthy of being discussed, in my view, especially for a company like JNJ, which typically delivers very reliable results.

Investors will likely focus on how management continues to navigate the loss-of-exclusivity (LOE) of its super-blockbuster Stelara. The biologic used to treat plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, ulcerative colitis, and Crohn's was responsible for 6.5% of JNJ's total sales in 2025. Stelara sales were down 41.5% year-over-year excluding currency effects, confirming fierce competition both from alternative treatments and biosimilars. It's the second-largest revenue contributor for JNJ, after oncology drug Darzalex, which has plenty of room to run, considering the U.S. composition of matter patent will expire in 2039. In 2025, Darzalex sales grew by 22% (again, excluding FX-effects), to $14.4 billion, or about 15% of total sales.

In my view, the continued strong performance of Darzalex in itself already relativizes the rather steep sales decline of Stelara. Also, investors should (and likely are, considering the valuation of JNJ stock) appreciate the fact that the company delivered adjusted operational sales growth of 4.2% - despite Stelara having represented a 6 %p+ headwind to sales in 2025.

To provide readers with a comprehensive overview, I compiled a summary of JNJ's key treatments (biologics and small-molecule drugs) in the table below, which includes the individual contribution to the Innovative Medicine segment sales, year-over-year growth/decline, U.S. sales contribution, and the expected LOE. In my view, Table 1 confirms that JNJ has a balanced portfolio with several blockbusters that are experiencing healthy growth. For the sake of brevity, I have not listed every single drug currently marketed by Johnson & Johnson, but since the treatments listed in the table account for nearly 90% of the Innovative Medicine segment's sales in 2025, I believe the data presented is sufficient to provide a good picture of the current quality of JNJ's drug portfolio.

In Figure 1, I illustrated the theoretical loss in sales due to LOEs for each year through 2036. Note, though that this illustration is based on the (unrealistic) assumption that sales of the respective drug drop to zero immediately after the LOE. Consequently, Figure 1 should be viewed merely as a rough approximation illustrating that Johnson & Johnson's LOEs are quite manageable, with the only concentration risk stemming from Darzalex.

Looking ahead, Johnson & Johnson appears well-positioned. In immunology, Icotyde – an oral treatment for plaque psoriasis – looks particularly promising. The peptide drug was approved as a first-line treatment only in mid-March this year. Also, I wouldn't underestimate Tremfya, which grew 40% in 2025, despite sales of already more than $5 billion. J&J's CEO expects the treatment could reach peak sales of $10 billion, so in line with Stelara – which peaked at $10.9 billion back in 2023 (see p. 25 of the 2023 10-K). Based on management's guidance, JNJ's oncology portfolio and the related pipeline could deliver segment growth of roughly 15% on an annualized basis through 2030, for example thanks to the recently approved combination treatment of Tecvayli and Darzalex Faspro for multiple myeloma or bladder cancer treatment Inlexzo (approved in September 2025).

MedTech (36% of 2025 sales) grew by 6.0% year-over-year on a reported basis (Figure 2), or by 4.3% on an adjusted operational basis (i.e. excluding acquisitions, divestitures and FX-effects). It looks like the acquisition of Shockwave (see my analysis of the transaction) is paying off, slowly but surely. Related sales essentially doubled year-over-year and came in at $1.15 billion. Abiomed, which was acquired in late 2022 at an enterprise value of almost $17 billion, remains somewhat disappointing. Sales growth was solid at 16% operationally, but the subsidiary is still rather small, with 2025 sales of just $1.75 billion (p. 28, 2025 10-K).

Taken together, J&J management's guidance of 6% revenue growth and 7% growth in adjusted EPS for 2026 seems quite realistic, and the path to $50 billion in oncology revenue by 2030 also appears achievable, though in my view it remains somewhat optimistic. Were it not for JNJ management's track record, I would have significant doubts about the realism of this goal. In any case, CEO Duato's remarks during the last earnings call left a very strong impression that reinforced my view.

Now, before assessing whether it could make sense to trim an overly large JNJ position after the rally, let's look at how free cash flow has developed in recent years, especially considering the split-off of the consumer health segment (now Kenvue Inc., KVUE).

As illustrated in Figure 2, free cash flow growth picked up nicely in 2025 and is more or less in line with earnings growth, pointing to sustained good earnings quality and cash conversion. On a side note, and as my long-term readers now, I always treat stock-based compensation (SBC) as if it were a cash expense, and the red bars in Figure 2 include adjustments for working capital movement for comparison purposes.

Conclusion: Time To Trim An Overly Large JNJ Stock Position Going Into Q1 Earnings?

2025 was indeed another solid year for Johnson & Johnson. Growth in both segments remains strong, and J&J's drug portfolio and pipeline suggest that management's growth expectations for the coming years are not overly ambitious. The MedTech division, of course, remains dependent on acquisitions as well as their successful integration and scaling. In this context, I continue to keep an eye on Shockwave and Abiomed, though I remain more positive on the former than the latter.

Given that JNJ stock has held up very well in recent weeks and appears to have stabilized around the $240 level, I would not bet on a knee-jerk downward reaction following an (unlikely) disappointing Q1 earnings report on Tuesday. That said, since my position in JNJ shares now accounts for nearly 6% of my portfolio value, I have been considering trimming my position by 10% to 20%.

From an operational perspective, I don't think selling JNJ shares makes sense. The company is performing very well, its balance sheet remains solid (e.g. net debt of just $28 billion, or roughly 1.5 times adjusted free cash flow), and the growth outlook is strong. However, based on the current consensus for 2026 EPS, JNJ stock is currently trading at a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 21 and a price-to-earnings-growth (PEG) ratio of 3. This is clearly well above the longer-term average valuation.

In my view, the market is (finally) appreciating JNJ's balanced portfolio and solid growth prospects. Furthermore, the talc litigation overhang appears to be a thing of the past. I have discussed this matter in previous articles and have consistently assessed the risk as manageable from the perspective of expected litigation costs.

Below, I've compiled an updated discounted cash flow sensitivity analysis that provides insight into current market expectations under certain assumptions.

Let's assume that JNJ can grow its free cash flow by 4% annually in perpetuity. Given the current consensus of average growth of >8% per annum through 2030, 4% is not an overly demanding growth rate. At this rate, JNJ stock would currently be fairly valued at a pre-defined cost of equity of 7.1%. This corresponds to an equity risk premium of 2.2% relative to the 30-year U.S. Treasury. That doesn't sound like much (and make no mistake, it really isn't much), but it's also not ridiculously low when we consider J&J's consistency in earnings growth, cash flow efficiency, and balance sheet quality. Compared to pure-play pharmaceutical companies, JNJ stock naturally looks very expensive, but I don't think it takes much explanation why this isn't your typical pharmaceutical company.

All in all, I still feel quite comfortable with the valuation of JNJ stock, even though it is clearly fully valued, if not modestly overvalued. However, I don't think the valuation premium is so high that it would justify reducing my position from a risk management perspective. If JNJ stock continues to rise without any concrete signs of further improvement in its growth outlook, I would consider trimming my position should the forward-P/E ratio approach the mid-20 range. Of course, and despite the current positive sentiment surrounding JNJ stock, one risk deserves special attention: the potential resurgence of concerns related to talc litigation in response to potentially several billion dollars that could be paid to plaintiff groups (purely hypothetically speaking).

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: IQMM: The $22 Billion Money Market ETF You Did Not Know About
data.description:
Thesis

When investors think about money market funds, they usually picture names priced at $1 per share that stay at that level over time. It used to be that way, and any 'breaking of the buck' would represent significant financial news. With the advent of retail-oriented trading platforms and exchange-traded funds, an entire suite of new products was derived that replaced traditional money market funds. And we are talking about T-Bill ETFs here, which came in full force.

However, money market funds have evolved, and they have managed to insert themselves into the ETF wrapper. We have reviewed one in the past, namely the iShares Prime Money Market ETF (PMMF). Today we are going to review a new entrant in the space that has amassed an astounding AUM exceeding $20 billion, and one that many investors have never heard of. And we are talking about the ProShares GENIUS Money Market ETF (IQMM) here.

What makes IQMM special?

Unlike traditional money market funds, IQMM is structured as an ETF; thus, it can be bought and sold throughout the day. But that is not the main selling point, especially when considering that iShares already came out with a competing product last year. What makes IQMM special is its compliance with the GENIUS Act:

The GENIUS Act (Guaranteeing Essential National Infrastructure in US Stablecoins) is the United States' first comprehensive legislation on stablecoins. This bill focuses on stablecoins, setting up regulatory guidelines for this form of digital currency across the United States. Stablecoin issuers must hold 1:1 reserves for any stablecoins issued. These reserves can be held in physical currency, US treasury bills, repurchase agreements and other low-risk assets approved by regulators. Issuers are required to report reserve composition and are subject to regular audits by registered public accounting firms.

Without getting too much into the crypto world structure and nomenclature, an investor needs to only understand that IQMM is now a regulatory compliant fund to hold required cash from the crypto universe. The ability to have a regulatory compliant ETF moves the crypto universe away from potential bank bankruptcies or other systemic financial system issues. It is not a surprise the fund's AUM is an astounding $22 billion now:

For those investors not very familiar with the crypto universe, suffice it to mention that one of the stablecoin providers had cash with Silicon Valley Bank (SVB), which went bankrupt:

In the early Saturday morning hours, the Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) failure caused USDC to de-peg from $1. The price dipped to almost $0.88 before recovering to around $0.96 at the time of writing. The reason for the de-peg is that Circle, the company issuing USDC, has $3.3 billion in reserves tied up at SVB. This is money backing the value of USDC, and if it was to be lost as part of SVB's failure, it would mean a financial hit for Circle.

The ability to achieve regulatory compliance via an ETF should not be underestimated, and we anticipate other providers of ETFs to jump in this arena shortly. The net expense ratio is 0.15%, which is in line with what other ETFs holding bills charge:

    • F/m US Treasury 3 Month Bill Fund ETF (TBIL) expense ratio: 0.15%
    • iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) expense ratio: 0.09%

    • State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) expense ratio: 0.13%

There is a fee waiver through January 2027, though, with the expense ratio going to 20 bps after.

Weekly payments and other analytics

The fund is very investor friendly, set-up with weekly payments and competitive yields:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 3.52%
  • Payment frequency: weekly
  • NAV strike: $100/share
  • Weighted average portfolio maturity: 1 month
  • Inception date: February 2026
  • AUM: $21.8 billion

Most T-Bill ETFs have monthly distributions and NAV levels that vary. We like the visual aspect for IQMM, which has a $100/share target NAV, thus reminding older investors of the 'buck' for money market funds structured in the mutual fund format.

No credit or duration risk

The ETF only holds T-Bills, thus has no credit risk. The fund is also structured with a very low duration, with the average portfolio maturity at 34 days. This translates into no duration risk either.

The fund is an elegant solution to a regulatory requirement, but it was structured smartly to have an easy-to-understand NAV, frequent distributions, and ultra-safe composition.

Given its size, the ETF comes with significant liquidity, which is set to move even higher. We anticipate much higher AUMs here until other fund managers come to the market with competing products.

While crypto universe treasurers are probably jumping with joy given the creation of IQMM, retail investors are also set to benefit from the massive liquidity provided by this ETF as well as its frequent weekly payments. The name comes with a competitive yield and fees, and thus is an appealing choice for parking cash.

Dollar parking for crypto

One of the initiatives of the current president resides with integrating the crypto universe within the financial system so that demand is created for dollars and treasuries. While we are not experts in crypto, having stablecoins pegged and required to hold dollar-based treasuries ensures a bid for dollars and the U.S.-centric approach to the global financial system. We have seen renewed calls from many BRICS countries for transactions to occur in Chinese yuan in order to move away from the regulatory framework imposed by the dollar, but little success in debunking the U.S. currency.

The GENIUS Act and funds like IQMM cement the importance of U.S. T-Bills and ensure the crypto framework is very well linked to the U.S. money market universe.

Conclusion

IQMM is a new money market ETF from ProShares. The fund came to market in February 2026 but already has assets in excess of $20 billion. The fund is compliant with the GENIUS Act and offers weekly interest payments. The ETF holds only T-bills with a very low duration, thus having virtually no credit or interest rate risk. The fund is structured with a visually appealing $100/share NAV, thus making it an appealing place to park cash.

data.title: GPIQ: Goldman Sachs Built The Income ETF I Wish Existed 5 Years Ago
data.description:
I have been investing in and writing about income-producing assets for a long time now. I have an entire segment of the portfolio structured around generating cash flow from equities. I spend quite a bit of time tracking and analyzing my income producing assets and I believe that the current market environment is an attractive entry point for long-term investors into income producing assets. The big thing about covered call strategy ETFs that people trend to glance over is that they are not conservative vehicles for people afraid of stocks just because they have a large distribution yield. They are structured to pay out a large amount of income for capping some of your upside during periods when nobody knows what is coming next. I believe that the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) is built for this exact moment. The Iranian conflict has caused the flow of roughly 20% of global oil supply to become constricted as the Strait of Hormuz has become the most important global choke point. We're headed into the heart of Q1 2026 earnings with big banks setting the stage for big tech later in the month. The market is going to demand proof that Big Tech AI spending is translating into real revenue causing uncertainty which could translate to increased options premiums. GPIQ is paying a distribution of $5.33 which is a yield of roughly 10.42% at a time when GPIQ is only down -2.7% for the year. I think that GPIQ will continue to generate low double digit or high single digit yields while returning positive appreciation throughout the year.

Following up on my previous article about GPIQ

Back in November I had written an article on GPIQ (can be read here) and since then the total return is 2.82% due to the large distribution compared to the S"&P 500 gaining 1.69%. I felt that GPIQ offered a compelling blend of capital appreciation and double-digit yield as the dynamic covered call strategy left the upside partially uncapped. This allowed GPIQ to benefit from market appreciation while producing a large recurring income stream for investors. Despite the evolving Fed policy GPIQ's distribution rate has been stable and I felt it had become more attractive in a failing rate environment. I am following up with a new article on GPIQ because I feel the current environment is creating opportunities for covered call ETFs especially with how uncertain the geopolitical landscape has been. In my opinion, GPIQ is built for periods like this as it can generate large amounts of recurring income when the market falls and follow the market higher on green days since a portion of the portfolio is uncapped.

What GPIQ Actually Is And How It Works

GPIQ launched in October 2023, so it does not have as long of a track record as some of the other covered call ETFs such as the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD). While this isn't a long enough timeframe for some investors what GPIQ does have is a clear and well executed strategy backed by Goldman Sachs Asset Management, and results that speak for themselves. GPIQ has constructed an equity portfolio that mirrors the Nasdaq-100 index. The top holdings look exactly like what you would expect with Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) representing 8.74% of the portfolio and Apple (AAPL) coming in at 7.67%. The tech sector makes up roughly 51% of the portfolio, with communication services at about 15% and 104 positions.

Where GPIQ gets interesting is the overlay segment of its strategy. It sells call options on anywhere between 25% and 75% of the underlying holdings which is the key differentiator. This is what Goldman calls a dynamic overwrite strategy which is significantly different than covered call ETFs operating at fixed percentages. QYLD writes calls on 100% of its portfolio every month while the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) utilizes equity-linked notes which have their own structural limitations. GPIQ can actively adjust how much of the portfolio is being overwritten based on market conditions which is what I like about it.

When volatility spikes and premiums expand, GPIQ has the ability to overwrite a larger portion of the portfolio. This allows them to collect larger premiums and deliver more income to shareholders. When the market trends higher GPIQ has the ability to reduce the overwrite percentage and let more of the underlying equity appreciation drive the share price higher. That flexibility is why GPIQ has managed to deliver both a double-digit yield and better total returns than most of its peers. GPIQ also utilizes FLEX options through the CBOE which are customizable exchange-traded option contracts. This gives the portfolio managers the ability to tailor strike prices and expiration dates in ways that standard listed options cannot match.

The taxed advantaged income profile is a benefit for income investors

GPIQ has paid $5.33 in distributions over the trailing twelve months (TTM) which is a 10.42% yield. The part that doesn't get enough attention is the tax treatment of these distributions. A large portion of the distributions get treated as return of capital because GPIQ uses index options that qualify for Section 1256 tax treatment. The gains are split on a 60/40 basis between long-term and short-term regardless of holding period. When I considered this against ETFs that are taxed as ordinary income GPIQ looks increasingly favorable especially at the end of the year when Uncle Same gets is fair share. For some investors this aspect can become extremely favorable especially when you have larger amounts of income being generated from the underlying investment.

Since the end of 2023 GPIQ has paid 29 monthly distributions which have amounted to $12.26 of income. GPIQ opened at $38.78 and since 2023 has appreciated by 32.70% while producing another 31.71% in monthly distributions. Since it went public GPIQ has generated a total return of 64.32%. When I look at the characteristics of GPIQ the dynamic income approach looks very appealing as the monthly distribution has averaged $0.42 over the past 29 months which is a monthly yield of 0.82% based on the current share price. From an income perspective this works in my portfolio and I am very bullish on GPIQ going forward because its structure will benefit when the geopolitical tensions finally get sorted out while maintaining an attractive yield compared to the risk free rate of return.

Why Earnings Season Makes The GPIQ idea Timely

Q1 2026 earnings season is about to get started as JPMorgan Chase (JPQ) will set the tone on Tuesday 4/14. Big tech will come right as we have the April FOMC meeting at the end of the month. The consensus expectations for the S&P 500 are about 13.2% YoY earnings growth for Q1. Technology has seen its YoY earnings growth rate increase to 45.1% from 34.3% on December 31st. The market is looking for another strong quarter from the names that dominate the Nasdaq-100 which would be beneficial for GPIQ. The March washout shook out a lot of the speculative froth causing a repricing of mega-cap tech. It looks like there has been a lot of AI fatigue and the market wants proof that the amount of capital being spent on CapEx is going to pay off. I think were setting up for a scenario where the companies that deliver strong results are going to get rewarded and we're going back to good news is good news and bad news is bad news. This kind of dispersion would be beneficial for GPIQ's covered calls strategy because it keeps implied volatility elevated across the index.

If the Magnificent 7 come in and report strong Q1 numbers, GPIQ would participate meaningfully in the upside because it can dial back its overwrite percentage. If earnings come in mixed and the market chops sideways or pulls back further it still has the ability to generate that 10% plus yield that everyone loves collecting. This is a setup where the fund gets paid either way and the only question is how much capital appreciation comes along with it. The reality is that nobody except the management teams know what these companies are going to produce and more importantly what they will guide for. What we do know is that the implied volatility embedded in Nasdaq-100 options right now is elevated relative to where it was for most of 2024 and early 2025. This has become a direct input as to how much income GPIQ can generate. When the VIX was sitting at 13 or 14, covered call funds had to work harder for their yield and now with the Vix over 20 the premiums are significantly larger.

GPIQ is outperforming it's peers since inception and it's not even close

I compared GPIQ to the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY), QYLD, JEPQ, and the Neos Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) since it's first day of trading and the results were overwhelmingly bullish. The metrics that I track are the starting price, todays price, how much appreciation was generated, the amount of income produced, and what the total return was. All of the data is in the table below. I am not as shocked as some may be to learn that GPIQ has almost doubled the total return of QYLD and been able to maintain a total annualized return that has almost matched the market.

Since inception GPIQ has generated $12.68 of capital appreciation while producing $12.68 in distribution income. This has led to a total return of 64.32% from a combination of 32.70% appreciation and 31.62% in distribution yield. The annualized return on GPIQ has been 22.37%. SPY which is the benchmark has a total return of 64.76% over this period with a 22.50% annualized return. GPIQ's performance is within a half of percent of SPY. QQQ which is the benchmark for the Nasdaq 100 has a total return of 71.97% with an annualized return of 24.65%. When I look at JEPQ, QQQI, and QYLD there is a huge drop off in total return since GPIQ hit the market. JEPQ has a total return of 53.18% while QQQI and QYLD both have the same total return at 36.30%. The data is clear and GPIQ is holding its own against the market and currently superior to its peers.

The risks to investing in GPIQ

Even though I am bullish on GPIQ there are several risks to consider. The first risk is concentration considering over 50% of this fund is in technology. If the Tech sector doesn't perform this earnings season we could experience a multi-quarter bear market in tech which will impact GPIQ's share price. The premium income provides a cushion but it's not going to save investors from a large step down in Nasdaq 100. . During the April 2025 tariff shock, GPIQ fell roughly 25% peak to trough before recovering. Next, if we get a ripping bull market due to the geopolitical tensions easing then GPIQ will underperform QQQ as the The covered call overlay inherently caps some upside. That is the trade-off as investors are trading some capital appreciation potential for current income. If your goal is pure growth maximization GPIQ isn't the right investment for you. The reality is that GPIQ has only been in existence for 2.5 years and we have not seen it navigate a prolonged recession or a sustained bear market. The dynamic overwrite strategy looks great in a volatile but ultimately recovering market but there is no data as to how its overwrite strategy will perform during a genuine downturn. Investors should do their own due diligence and make sure this strategy is right for them.

Conclusion

One of the things I think about is if I was going to build building an income-focused portfolio from scratch today would I want a vehicle that gives me exposure to the best companies while paying a double-digit yield that is tax-advantaged? The answer is yes and GPIQ checks off all the boxes especially in this market environment. We are in a market where volatility is elevated and earnings season is about to inject a fresh round of uncertainty. The Fed is not likely to cut rates and the geopolitical landscape is a mess. All of those factors push implied volatility higher which pushes option premiums higher and positively impacts GPIQ's distribution income. GPIQ doesn't need optimal conditions or a rising market to work and it's built for the types of uncertain environments we're living through. I am still bullish on GPIQ and adding to my position.
